

Shawn (00:00:00):

Hey, it’s André and Shawn today. It’s week three of the Art of Email Workshop. The topics for this week are supposed to be value newsletter, bridge emails, and story power promotions. We’re going to cover those three topics, and we’re going to do a little preview for the PLS, that module is released today, actually. So, we’ll be talking at about it next week, but we have a PLS coming up. So, we thought we’d just take this opportunity and we’d give you kind of a preview of what it’s like to be thinking about a product launch series almost a month before it happens.

And we haven’t had this conversation yet, actually. So, we want to get kind of a window into some of the really initial thinking that goes into a PLS. But before we talk about that, let’s jump up and start talking about a value newsletter. What I’ve done is we’re looking at a list of questions and we’re just going to go through them and see where they lead.

So, the first question really is, very broadly speaking, how do we think about, and how should our listeners think about creating a value newsletter? I’ll kick this off with just a comment from a conversation I had with a really good friend on Friday.

Using the example, and I don’t know if anyone listening to this is familiar with Tim Ferriss’ 5-Bullet Friday email newsletter. Overall, I’ve subscribed to it for years. The person I was talking to, his name is Aaron, subscribed to it for years too, but the comment that he made and we kind of joked around about is that when he gets this newsletter, he doesn’t really know what he is supposed to do with it.

Sometimes, there’s a link to a documentary or there’s a quote, or there’s a podcast episode, and what ends up happening, and the joke that I made when we were talking about is, oftentimes, when I get through a 5-Bullet Friday email, and there’s nothing that I need to do, I feel a sense of relief. I’m like, “Oh, good. I don’t have to figure this out.” I really appreciate everything that Tim Ferriss is doing. I’ve listened to more Tim Ferriss interviews than any other podcaster. I think what he is doing is incredibly valuable, but it is interesting that a newsletter, it …

A newsletter creates a cognitive response and an emotional response. And being aware of that, it’s really an important. What impact does this have on the reader? Do they know what to do? Is it too much, too little? Is it easy to ignore because there’s really not much there? Is it easy to ignore because there’s way too much there?

I don’t know where that Goldilocks sweet spot is. How do you think about this, André? How do you think about finding that middle ground where the value newsletter, it’s doing what it’s supposed to do, but it’s not doing too much or too little or too frequent, too infrequent? I’m just curious to hear your thoughts on this.

André (00:03:03):

Yeah. I mean, it’s never going to be a one size fits all. It’s about aligning with how you want to show up in the world. Our daily newsletter is a reflection of stuff that interests us and the level of depth that we enjoy writing about. But it takes us a significant amount of time to write our newsletters for the most part, because they’re quite … It requires us to think on the page and then clarify our thinking. That’s a resource that we are willing to put in because it lights us up, but it’s not for everybody.

Other people, they just love researching and just consuming stuff. For them, they have perhaps curated a certain type of audience that they enjoy those sorts of things. So, that person becomes like a conduit, like a middle man that’s done the work of filtering through the noise and trying to find a little signal. And then that gets put into a value newsletter in … Tim Ferriss has done it. His framing is the 5-Bullet Friday.

We receive some of the newsletters and it’s not a 5-Bullet Friday, it’s a 500-bullet Friday. It can get ridiculous. And then there’s Harvard versions and we’ve cited a few in the module, the ones that we enjoy. Even the ones that we enjoy, I mean, for instance, we’ve been working really hard over these last few weeks delivering this course as we’re shooting this recording. And we are only, what, halfway through this now.

One of the newsletters that I really enjoy is from Kyle Westaway. He’s the lawyer, attorney. But in my inbox, I think I’ve got five of his email. He sends them every Saturday. I’ve got five weeks worth of his email sitting in my inbox, because I don’t have the time to read them just yet, but I don’t want to click on them because I’m using email client that when you click on it and look at it, it then disappears. There’s these different layers of stuff we do to each other, but anyway.

I still want to read that, but it’s going to get to a point when there’s maybe seven of those things stacked up and I’m just … I don’t have the bandwidth, so I’m going to just mark them as read and then just draw a line and direct that I’m going to start to read them again when they start to come in again. But then there’s other emails that are just easy to read. I can just quickly, “Oh, so there’s five of them that I haven’t seen. No problem. I’ll just quickly go through them.”
Dense Discovery is one of those that come out every Tuesday that we’ve cited in the module. Yeah. They’re short. I can get through them really easily and it’s always valuable. So, I think there’s just that balance and there’s never going to be a perfect fit.

Shawn (00:06:44):

We see this question occasionally and there’s some variation of it too. Some variation of so and so says, “I should send …” Whatever, it’s usually like, “Ben Settle says I should send emails every day. Should I do a daily newsletter?” And it’s like, “Well, who knows?”

I mean, it really depends on, first of all, how much time do you have. How much does your audience want to hear from you? How much are they here? There’s no right answer to that question. And really, the way I think to think about it is really, you have to start by defining what is valuable to your audience related to your area of expertise, or interests, or experience, whatever it is.

And then what’s the nature of that value? Is that something somebody wants to … Ben Settle is an example. I don’t read his emails, but I have read his emails, but I think his audience is reading that email every day because it’s part of their professional life for many of them. It can be inspiration. It can be interesting. Lots of things. Makes perfect sense in that scenario.

But if your audience isn’t engaging with you single day, that doesn’t make sense for them, then that format wouldn’t make sense. There is no prescriptive answer here. It really is taking the time to take a step back and ask yourself how does what I do fit in with what my audience wants and gets value from. And then what frequency and what depth makes sense? Is it a lot of shallow things, one deep thing, lots of deep things?

And then once you kind of get an idea for that, it’s just a matter of experimenting. We’ve experimented. I think before my arrival, you did … I don’t know if they call them semi regular, but I don’t think you had a very specific schedule for at least the one period of time. And then we switched over to biweekly, fortnightly, and then we went to weekly and I suspect we were sending newsletters weekly over the summer, so it’s hard to tease out whether it was summer or an increased frequency.

But I suspect the combination of weekly emails with a lot more in depth content probably, at least some percentage of our audience, was too much and we’ve since gone back to biweekly. So, who knows? There’s no right answer here.

Just to continue on with the VNL, let’s talk about themed series versus one-offs. If I’m an Art of Email customer and I’m thinking to myself, “When do I just send a newsletter versus when do I put two or more together into a themed series?” We’re going to talk about a use case for that later, but just in general, how do you think about the themed series versus the one-off?

André (00:09:53):

Yeah. I mean, from our perspective, we have an idea, I think, of topics we would like to cover over the course of a year. And within the context of that year, there will be X number of product launch series that we’re planning to put out because of this open and close enrollment, like a Momentum [Bunny 00:10:21] workshop. So, that all factors in.

I think one of the things that we think about is so long as we are covering all the topics that we know that our audience care about and we want to share, there’s going to be certain times where theming something is going to make more sense. Or if a certain topic needs to be covered at a certain depth that can’t be done in a single VSO, VNL.

Most of our emails are quite dense anyway between 1,000 and 2,000 words. And if there’s something that we want to say that’s even deeper than that, well sending out a 5,000 word email is probably not going to solve the problem. There’s going to be less value on that, because it’s just going to be too cognitively dense. We will then break that out over a number of newsletters.

Coming up towards a promotion, that makes sense that there’s certain things we want to cover. We can theme that. We’ll mention our Black Friday thing coming up. We have some ideas for that and there’s a certain theme that makes sense that we talk about. Even for ourselves just to get into that mindset of deep diving into that thing for us to think about that suddenly to switch gears and think more deeply about that subject.
It’s nice when we are writing about it, because then it’s just flicking us into that mode. And then we transition straight into the promotion and it just flows nicely. It creates some level of tension in some cases if that’s intentional for us. Do you have any ideas, any more on theming?

Shawn (00:12:31):

Yeah. A theme is a good thing to have in the toolbox. I think you covered the reasons to do it. I suspect we’re going to do a 5,000 word email when we get to the end of the year, which we’ll talk about in a second. In general, we don’t want people to read a chapter from Warren Peace in one of our email. So, to break something out over weeks, also to break something out over weeks to pull attention forward in this summer. We’re recording this in 2021.

In the summer of 2021, we did a four-part series called Courage and Perseverance over four weeks. There’s a lot in that series. Way too much for a single … It could be a mini course effectively, but it pulled attention from each of those emails from beginning to end through the entirety of it.

In that sense, that series could be almost converted into a soap opera series, because it has all of the ingredients of serialized narrative in it to main attention. There’s so many reasons to do this. Sometimes, the one-offs just makes sense. That’s a good segue into the next thing on the list here, which is sometimes there’s content you want to create. There’s an idea. When it’s jumping into my mind also from this summer was a simple testing framework. That came out of a segment from Sesame Street, but it was one of those things when you hear it, you’re like, “That really maps well to a very complicated thing that our audience could understand in a way that’s much easier. So, let’s create that.”

It’s a gift to the audience. Here’s a way to take something complicated and make it simpler. 1,500 words, 2,000 words, whatever it was. That segues well into this next idea, which is sometimes the content that we’re creating, we don’t want it to just disappear. It was in a newsletter or it was an email a year ago or something that we want to repurpose that content to further expand the world that we’re building for our audience.

It creates organic entry points into our world. It creates externally facing value for our audience, whether or not they’re paying customers. They can be new entries into our world or somebody who’s been with us for a long time. But by taking the content that we’re creating foreign newsletter changing it slightly to remove the specific references to the email or any of the things we preface, and then putting that on our website and saying, “Hey, this is the type of thing that we create with our emergent marketing series. If you are interested and more like this, then you can sign up.”

So, it becomes effectively … We don’t like to use the term lead magnet, because we’re not offering something behind an opt-in wall or putting it right out front and saying, “Hey, if you like this, we do lots of stuff like this and sign up for our email.” It’s very little extra work to have to do that.
Yeah, I think theme and world building, they seem to be related in many ways. Although now that I’m thinking about it, most of what … We have some multi-part series on the site now. Chefs and Cooks was over two parts. The theme can work for world building or not. Those two things are related. Anything on topic of world building that you want to add from the value newsletter?

André (00:16:16):

We did a series for the general business that played out over five emails, I think it was. That’s been repurposed onto the website that’s linked to in the lesson. I think, actually, one of the things that’s worth drawing attention to that we haven’t explicitly laid out in the course yet. Maybe now is a good time to mention it is there’s a Harvard version that you can do with the VNL.

This is just an example. Let’s say you’ve got two and a half thousand words, 3,000 words. There’s a 3,000 word idea you want to unpack. One of the things you could do is you could put 500 words of that in the VNL and it has all the context. It’s their entire lead in. There’s all sorts of tension pulling in people that are interested in this idea. And then it kind of ends with the rest of this is on the website.

Well, in fact, the entire single piece would be on the website minus for the little nuances and context that only makes sense inside of the VNL. That will be on the website. There’s these two pieces. So now, you’ve got people that are receiving your emails and they’re getting the value and the pull. This feel of tension, pulling them to want to discover this thing, because it’s interesting to them and they’ve got to click to get it, because it’s on the website. And then you can have videos and inline images that make more sense for that to live on the website.

But now, you’re getting people to click. This is an idea that we’ve done many times in the past. It’s a nice way to condition your email subscribers to click. And obviously, clicking on links is a signal that Apple can’t even take away with the stripping out or the tracking. That can always be tracked. It’s a level of engagement that’s black and white, and it’s nice to have. That’s another nuance way of thinking about some of your newsletters that might make sense.

Shawn (00:18:52):

Yeah. I think the last thing on the newsletter here that we’ll talk about, and I’m not even sure how best to position this. I’ll position it from my perspective and then I’m curious to hear your perspective on it.

Last year, we did a year in review email. I don’t necessarily even remember what led up to that. I think it was just one of us said it. I think you said it in a conversation. We knew the year was coming to an end and it was like, “Hey, let’s look back and see what we created.” And it was staggering how much we created. I think, I can’t remember if it was 75,000 words of content. Two or three free courses. It was insane how much we had created. And then we appended in a PS. I think you did a PS of books and I did a PS of podcasts that really resonated with me. It was very much a mix.

What I like about this year end review, and to get the value of this, you don’t actually have to create a year end review email. But to think about it as an idea, I think it’s really powerful. Because I know throughout the entirety of 2021, in the back of my mind when we’re creating, I’ve had this thought many, many times where I’m like, “I can’t wait to write the year end review and really to compile everything we’ve done this year into some sort of coherent overview.”
For me, at least, it’s been incredibly inspiring and motivating. We’ve said already a few times today that no plan survives contact with reality. But our plan is to send our last emergent marketing newsletter on December 3rd. Which knowing that, now we’re I think 39 days away from that or something I realized this morning, writing morning pages. Now, it’s coming into focus. It’s like, “Oh, okay. We have this book end year where we’re going to get to go back and look and see not only what have we done this year, but what really have we done from the perspective of our newsletter. What did we publish in our newsletter this year and other things that we did?”

I don’t know why that’s so inspiring to me, but it’s such a wonderful way to bring one year to a close and then get excited about the year that follows. We haven’t really talked about how … There are lots of things that we could do structurally in this. There are lots of ways we can approach it. There are lots of ways we could have a PS and add some texture to it. But for some reason, that’s been very inspiring.

And I think people listening to this think about that as an option, even if you don’t do it. Because you may get to the end of the year and like, “Oh, wow, I don’t have the bandwidth for this.” But keeping that in mind, it’s a nice way to look back and see everything that you’ve accomplished. We’ve never talked about the year end review idea. I’m curious to hear your take on how that fits into your thinking.

André (00:22:09):

I love the idea of the year end review. I’ve seen other people do versions where they’ll do it in January. They’ve kind of had their years done. They’ve enjoyed their holiday and used the holiday to reflect back. And then in the January, they essentially do their previous year end review. I’ve seen that happen for us. We used it as a forcing mechanism to do it in the moment, reflecting back before any holidays.

There’s just so many downstream benefits, some of which are obvious, some of which are not in doing something, laugh a year end review. One of which is any people coming into your world can look at these single emails and these single emails, they’re like a table of contents that just point in these different directions about stuff that was recently done over the course of a year. And then when you’ve got many, like a stack of year end reviews to point to, it literally goes everywhere. You can use those year end review emails. I mean, especially if they’ve been archived on your website, which is, I think, that’s where they should be.
When somebody gets to the end of an IBS, it’s a great thing to point them to you, because they’ve been through this little journey, there’s this relationship being established. Point them to the year end review and, boom, they’ve just got access to all of these things that you’ve done over the previous year, and maybe even link to a few of them. That just goes a long way in reestablishing that relationship in just a single click and then just looking at that stuff. It’s a rabbit hole.
I mean, it’s an email full of rabbit holes. That’s exactly what this world building thing is all about. It’s about creating doorways into these little rabbit holes that people get lost in. And before they know it, they’ve consumed thousands and thousands of words of content or videos or podcasts or whatever. That’s essentially done a huge chunk of the relationship building stuff right there.

Shawn (00:24:38):

I was just thinking in 2030, we can do a decade end review. It will be the longest [crosstalk 00:24:43] we’ve ever written. You heard it here first. All right. I’m going to transition to the second question. This is probably the question we’ve seen the most in the comments, so I’m looking forward to answering this definitively.

It’s not so much what’s the difference. Let’s think about it in terms of when should someone use a hard bridge versus a soft bridge. We need some to do some work in the clarifying on that, but be before we add that to the module, how should workshop participants think about the hard bridge versus the soft bridge?

André (00:25:20):

I think I’m going to put my neck on the line and say that every single bridge email should be a hard bridge email, except for one use case, which is the soft. That’s just wrapped up in some context. Otherwise, I think every bridge email, unless I’m missing something here, should really be a hard bridge. Because we are asking people to raise their hand for something that’s going to happen after that. And we never want to presume that just because they’ve opted in at some point in time, we now have caught the launch over their attention and their permission to send them stuff forever. I think every bridge email is a hard bridge email, except for this one use case, which we can talk about. What do you think about that?

Shawn (00:26:24):

That’s a good question. I guess I can think of a few scenarios. They’re probably all the same scenario, though, where you use a hard bridge. I’m thinking when we did the Durable Business series, it was the fall 2020 enrollment for three courses for, at the time, autoresponder madness sphere of influence and the traffic engine. We did a five-part series that was about awareness, engagement and conversion. High level, no reference to our products.

If I’m not mistaken, everybody got that. That to me would be like an opportunity to use a soft bridge and say, “Hey, next week we’re doing this five-part series. We think it’s valuable for everybody.” At the end of that five-part series …

I mean, we were transparent about it too. We said that five-part series is half of a 10-part series, but the next five parts, the second five parts, we’re going to specifically talk about how our courses fit into these ideas, and you have to raise your hand for that. So, that was a soft bridge to hard bridge transition.
I could talk myself into saying that we could have used a hard bridge on the first one too, certainly. I think I’m okay with the fact that we use. I’m obviously okay with it. I like that there was a soft bridge. I just said, “Hey, this is coming up next week.” And if someone chose ignore it, I don’t know that we’ve taken too much from them. I think easy to delete. Yeah.

I could also imagine soft bridge to … I mean, for me, the way I think about the distinction between a soft bridge and a hard bridge is a hard bridge separates trust from revenue always. If there’s revenue on the other side of a link, then it’s a hard bridge. If there’s revenue on the other side of a hand raising, then that’s a hard bridge, because we’re not going to show somebody an offer unless they’ve said they’re interested. But information, when the intent is to …

Let’s assume that we put together a three-part series that’s just generally useful. I like a soft bridge to say, “Hey, next week, we’re going to a Monday, Wednesday, Friday. Next week, we’re going to this three-part series on X, Y, Z. We think you’ll be interested in it.” A soft bridge there feels fine. I feel like we’ve earned the right to do that with our audience, but I wouldn’t do that if within those things, they could buy something. If we were promoting something. I don’t know if that’s in line with how you’re thinking about it. I’m curious what your use case is that you’re thinking about through the soft bridge.

André (00:29:35):

Yeah, I agree. And I’m glad you brought up that use case. Because of what we were sending out, it was so in line with the broad needs and why our subscribers are paying attention to us is the reason why we sent out that series, it was relevant to everybody. If what we were saying in that thing was not relevant anymore, then those people could have unsubscribed. And we got unsubscribes. We definitely got unsubscribes.

In that case, I think it’s fine. It’s almost like an extension of a value newsletter that’s themed. But again, just want to be clear that because it’s easy to do soft bridges doesn’t mean it should be a soft bridge, just because there’s no revenue on the other side. Shawn made a good point, and I love that way of thinking of it, that if there is revenue, at least from our perspective, it needs to be a hard bridge. There’s a million other versions where there’s no revenue, but it still should be a hard bridge.

If you’ve written a written a soap opera series, most soap opera series should really be people raising their hand to get this thing. Not just because there’s no revenue in a certain soap opera series, but because people raising their hand, it’s focusing their attention on this thing that’s about to happen as opposed for us just to presume that the entire email list now has … We have their attention, like 100% of them, which is not the case ever.

These hard bridges is just recognizing that at any point in time, we don’t have the attention of every single person on our email list. Even if this thing is quite relevant for them, they might just be in some scenario that they’re just not going to pay attention. And then just having those emails stack up in the inbox isn’t a great thing. So, just be mindful of the fact that if you just think about it like a mental thing that, well, maybe every bridge should be a hard bridge unless. And that unless needs to come with just making sure that all the boxes are ticked and there’s no reason why it shouldn’t be a hard bridge and it can be a soft bridge, whereas it’s relevant for the broad majority of people in this moment.

It’s kind of like a gray area, but just respect the fact that we don’t have the attention of 100% of people on our email list. And the bridges recognize the fact that, that’s the case and that’s why we ask people and then they raise their hands and then it focuses their attention on this thing that’s going to happen. Those people that didn’t raise their hands for something in this scenario, maybe a soap opera series, they get to raise their hand next time. In fact, even for the same soap opera series, there could be a second go at it in the context of a world that we built. Just because they don’t raise their hand to go into the scene now, they can go into some other time. We shouldn’t just presume that everybody should get this thing.

Shawn (00:33:22):

I always feel compelled to say. Nothing is absolute because they’re … I keep thinking back to the one time we, me violated the hard bridge rule. 2020, your birthday promotion, we sent an offer to everyone without a hard bridge. If someone’s going to see that and be like, “See, they they do …”

I want to at least talk about why we did that, because a lot of thinking went into that. Partly, it was timing. I’ll just blame André for telling me late that it was his birthday or whatever. The nature of that promotion, there was a really interesting story around it. The story being that André sort of mentioned his birthday. We usually do something and there were all these things we couldn’t do.

So, I’m like a fool who said, “Oh, I’ll take care of it. I’ll come up was something.” In the process of creating an offer, I had to go back and really rely on and have conversations with others, but really had to go back to the basics of what makes a good offer. If you go back and you see that original promotion, yes, it was sent to everybody.

No, there was no hard bridge, but the critique I got from a lot of friends who are direct response marketers were like, “Wow, you really needed to get to the point here.” You kind of buried the offer at the end and it was like, “Right, because the main point of that email that we really wanted that Friday email in particular was a newsletter. We really wanted to deconstruct the offer creation process. And then the offer that was created followed that process.”

The idea was somebody could read that email top to bottom, have no interest whatsoever in the offer itself, but it was essentially a mini masterclass on offer creation. And then we gave everybody something for free that fit in on that Sunday, and it was kind of over Monday. I don’t want to say violated this rule. We have shown an offer to our entire audience at least once, but it wasn’t something that we did sort of wantingly. “Whatever, we can do this once in a while.”

It was very much like if we’re going to do this, we have this tremendous responsibility to make sure it is as valuable as possible for everyone who’s not at all interested that they could save it for later, come back and look at it, think, “Wow, this was really interesting and really useful.” Independent of they didn’t have to do anything to get value. And that may be a further distinction.

The last thing we want to do is open the door to people and there are all these scenarios where you can just send everybody an offer. That’s not the point, but the point is that in rare circumstances, when there’s so much value embedded in what you’re doing that there’s an exception to every rule. Or maybe that’s just me rationalizing the mistake, but we’ll leave it at that. All right. Let’s move on.

André (00:36:46):

One of the things we didn’t discuss is the use case outside of what we’ve just … All these gray areas that we’ve just spoken about is the one scenario where we think that it’s always going to be okay. We’ve drawn attention to this inside of the training anyway. If there’s a product specific RBS. So, let’s just use Art of Email as an example. Somebody goes to the product page, and there’s an opportunity for them to buy up, but they choose not to. And they add themselves to the email sequence around Art of Email. Well, that’s a signal to us that they’re interested, but they’re not ready to buy yet.

Them opting in is the hard bridge. It’s them essentially raising their hand in that moment that, “I want this thing. I want to know more about this thing.” So in that case, in that scenario, that obvious can transition. Once it’s done at this thing, it can transition to a story-powered promotion, for example, that doesn’t ask people to re-raise their hand again, because they’ve just done that.

You can’t extend this infinity out and stick all sorts of little things in between, because now it’s gone too long. For them, raising their hand about something that’s very specific and it’s around a product, then that class as a hard bridge, them opting in, and then you can move straight from an obvious to a story-powered promotion. We think that’s [crosstalk 00:38:36].

Shawn (00:38:37):

Maybe we need a third term. Maybe there’s soft bridge, which is you’re going to a none … Someone is going from something to something else that doesn’t sell and that transition is just letting them know. Then there’s a hard bridge that they have to raise their hand. And then this third scenario is like an embedded bridge, which is like they’ve raised their hand at the beginning and said, “Hey, I’m interested in that thing.”

We already know they’re interested in the course or whatever it is, and we emphasized the relationship first and then one of the emails transitions to … Just for the sake of argument, say we have four emails that it’s talking about the thing. It’s establishing empathy about the need for whatever the course, whatever it is, the history of it, how we came to create it. There’s no link to sell it.

Yeah. Maybe in the PS, they can buy it directly or whatever, but when we make that shift into why they would want to buy it and what it will do for them, and all of the things that a typical story-powered promotion. That transition, the thing that goes from one to the other, it confused everyone. It’s like an embedded bridge. We’re just kind of making that splash.

Because it is. It’s critically important to draw attention to the fact that we’re not doing this for the sake of making people jump through hoops. We’re looking for the signal. And when someone says, “Hey, I want to know more about this course.” Well, that’s a very strong signal. That’s a seven, eight, nine, 10 above their head in that diagram. They’ve already done that, but we’re still respecting them and we’re still focusing on the relationship and we’re still having some kind of transition that’s …

Effectively, this isn’t how we would word it, but effectively saying, “Now that you kind of understand the background and sort of where this fits in, we’re going to start talking about how this can be helpful for you specifically.” And then off we go with a story-powered promotion, and that is a soft bridge. It’s a soft bridge or an embedded bridge or whatever you want to call it on the back of explicit permission to talk about the course. We didn’t trick anybody into thinking that we weren’t going to be talking about a course. I like that distinction. I think that’s very valuable. Okay, [crosstalk 00:41:04].

André (00:41:03):

Now that, that’s crystal clear, we can move on.

Shawn (00:41:06):

Right. We get 200 more questions. What’s an embedded bridge? All right. That’s a perfect segue into moving on to the third topic, which is story-powered promotions. Maybe my fear about story-powered promotion is completely unfounded. When I say my fear, my fear for the audience really is how easy it is to get caught and tripped up on the word story. It’s like, “Oh, I don’t have any good stories to tell. I don’t know what I should use for a story for my story-powered promotion.” Stare at the blank screen, which is hard enough, anyways.

When I think about a story-powered promotion, you don’t need a lot to go on. You could have just read a book and something in that book. It really resonated for you, and that kind of frames everything that falls. It could be a movie. It could be a scene in a movie. That’s just a particularly poignant scene in the movie.
It could be a quote, an anecdote, some vignette of experience that you’ve had. Like all of those things can set up. They can set up the story and the story-powered promotion. As long as you connect that inspiration to frame the story. I think the example in the Art of Email, I’m trying to remember where it is. I guess it’s part one of the story-powered promotion.

The person who visited you in Gibraltar, the way that is set up, that’s a perfect example of a little vignette experience of something that happened that sets up the larger point. And then you just ran with that larger point, became the spine of everything that followed. To me, that’s a great example of a story-powered promotion that has almost like a seed that gets planted and that seed then ties things. It sprouts or ties things together. How do you think about the inspiration and getting away from the story, capital S story idea of story-powered promotions?

André (00:43:20):

I’m going to cheat slightly and I’m going to suggest a book. It’s called Storyworthy. I think it’s Matthew Dicks, if I remember correctly, but it’s definitely called Storyworthy. At the end of every chapter, he’s giving practical things you can do to program yourself or to get into the routine of being able to capture stories. It’s done so well. He’s got different techniques. He teaches.

But if you just read that and just did some of the things he talks about, it’s such an amazing way of capturing things that may not seem significant in the moment, but whenever you need a story, you’re going to have more stories that you know what to do with. So, I think that would be the first thing to do.
One of the things that I do and Shawn does is we consume lots of information, we are curious. So, we’re always reading and listening. I’m capturing my things into my PKM. One of the things I do in my personal knowledge management tool that I’m using is I’m capturing all the things I’m finding, creating some context that matters to me. I’m tagging things. So, if it’s got anything to do with Art of Email, for example, or a SOS, I’ll tag something as an SOS.

Then later, when I’m looking for stories, I can just go to my PKM and just look at my SOS page and all the references that I thought in the moment was something worth using as a story, I can then just reference. So, it’s just something that you’ve got to do all the time, and then you’re always going to have stories.
And again, this doesn’t have to be story stories, but they can be. When you don’t have a story in the moment because you decided to create a story-powered promotion, that’s when it becomes slightly stressful, but there’s no reason why. I mean, there’s stories everywhere and it doesn’t have to be your stories. It could be things that have happened to other people, interesting things. Yeah.

Shawn (00:46:01):

What you just said, I think, is exactly it, there’s stories everywhere, just everywhere. As you were talking, I was thinking about the various podcasts I’ve listened to recently, just the books. I mean, you name the conversations of the two interviews just jumped into mind where somebody said something. And just a sentence, a single sentence or a tiny little vignette, you could hang an entire promotion on.

When we think about story, I think what we tend to do is we think about like the Joseph Campbell version of story where we’re like, “Okay, what could I think of where there was a character who had to overcome a challenge to defeat the dragon and save the princess?”

When we’re talking about stories for a story-powered promotion, the audience is the one who’s going … They’re the ones going through that experience of having something to overcome. The audience is the hero. They’re having to do that work. And what the story that we’re setting up, it’s a way to engage their attention. It’s a way to frame something in a way that’s understandable or often with a metaphor, or something that’s easy to understand.

Metaphors communicates so much. They could be pop culture metaphors. I mean, if we use the phrase, “Use the force, Luke.” Most people know what that means. Most people know it’s a reference from Star Wars. It has a certain pop culture reference. There’s so many ways that you can use these little elements to then expand on. Or if you have them, tell a story.

That’s one of the things I added to the list to talk about here is I want to create a story-powered promotion for the traffic engine. If you can imagine how to do that, I can sit down and I can think about stories that relate specifically to the topic of the traffic engine. So, if you’ve read the manifesto, if you see it on the site and you’ll see some references to … I can’t remember what the award was, best marketing idea or something.

I was in Todd Brown’s top one, which is his exclusive mastermind and there’s this contest. I think we had one minute to pitch. I think that’s what it was. We had one minute to pitch an idea, and then everybody voted on the certain number, and then you had 20 minutes to explain what it was. And then everyone in the group voted and went, “Well, I want that.”

That’s a story. And then from that, one of the people who was there at that meeting was Kevin Rogers who runs Copy Chief. And Kevin pulled me aside and said, “Hey, would you like to speak this June at John Carlton’s group, his platinum mastermind? I think they usually have a subject matter expert.” And I just reached out to John and said, “Hey, I think you’d be a great guest.” Of course, the answer is ever … “Do you want to hang out with John Carlton?” The answer is always yes. There’s like a story, then another story. Then I went and I spoke at the Carlton thing and got a great response. That’s a story.

They’re all of those stories, but then there’s all the stories that have nothing to do with the traffic engine from the content of it. They have nothing to do with me talking about that content or me running an agency for 21 years. None of that. It could be a story about something that paid traffic is like.
I think the first email in the original series for the traffic engine for the PLS was about American Airlines makes more money selling credit cards than they make flying people all over the world. That sounds crazy, but it’s true. That’s an interesting story. It was an interesting frame, because the way I connected that is that American Airlines is aggregating audiences. They’re physically aggregating them seats and then Facebook and Google aggregate audiences differently.

That’s a story about an airline that’s weirdly interesting. I mean, who would believe that an airline makes more money on credit cards than it does on flying people around? That seems so counterintuitive. That’s weird, and then how it fits into paid traffic is weird. And it’s sort of interesting, and it pulls attention forward.

There’s stories everywhere. And when something kind of pops out, like someone says something and you’re like, “That’s interesting.” This is a ridiculous story to tell, but this to me is kind of how I know things are interesting. I remember this is 10 or 12 years ago. I was in a Starbucks and the table behind me, they were having a conversation about it was a holiday coming up in the US. And the one person said to the other, :Hey, are you going to so and so’s this weekend?” He said, “No, I kind of had a rough weekend last weekend.” And the guy said, “Well, what happened?” He said, “Well, we were supposed to go out on the boat and fish, but I got there early and we started drinking and we soon realized we were too drunk to fish.”

Now, this is 12 years ago that this happened. But I remember sitting there thinking to myself, and I did eventually. I turned around and asked them. I said, “Hey, I don’t mean to intrude, but I’ve been fishing many times in my life and I’ve had something to drink many times in my life, but I don’t remember ever being too drunk to fish. What the hell is that like?”

You’re always paying attention from these little things that happen. André captures them in the PKM system, you can capture them in a notebook, you can capture them anywhere, but that’s the goodness of life. Those little things that happen that are everywhere. Now, you’re wondering, André, what it’s like to be too drunk to fish, aren’t you?

André (00:52:08):

I am. Yeah, totally. I need to start fishing.

Shawn (00:52:14):

What I think that illustrates, though, I mean, irrespective of the … It’s just sort of funny, but sometimes a story is just one thing that doesn’t make sense, or one thing that’s just interesting by itself, a sentence. Like, “Wait a minute.” And then you just run with it. That’s the stake in the ground. Any other thoughts on the story of powered … I mean, this is something we both love so much. We can talk about this for days.

Thinking about from the audience’s perspective, how does someone go about writing that first story-powered promotion? Let’s assume they have something that they’ve sold. They know the offer converts. They’ve created the relationship building series, and now they want to create, let’s say for the sake argument, a five to seven email story-powered promotion. If you’re taking somebody by the hand, how would you help them write that?

André (00:53:20):

Okay. So, let me think this out loud. I’m just looking inside of my notes here, my Apple notes over the weekend. I’ll use this as an example, but I haven’t thought through what I would do with this yet, but this is just an idea of an idea that’s not a story, but it can turn into a story.

I was listening to Jordan Peterson over the weekend, an interview between him and Dr. Oz. And at 49 minutes, 38 seconds, I made a note of something he said that I thought was profound from our point of view and something that could lead into something more significant later on. So, I took a screenshot, which is what I normally do on my phone, because I’m listening to the podcast or the YouTube video on my phone. And he said, “You can’t bend the structure of reality. Everything you distort snaps back.” Then he just went on. He unpacked that idea a little bit more, and then he moved on to the next thing. It felt like something that was worth capturing, which I did.

Now, thinking out loud at this point, because I haven’t gone beyond the unpacking really digging into what he’s just said, the implications of that. If Shawn and I were going to write a story-powered promotion for Sphere of Influence, that could be something that we start email one with right in the very beginning. It was Saturday and I was having lunch and I’m listening to my podcast, which is what I do every Saturday. This becomes part of the story now. I’m just rifting now in real time.

And then I listen to this thing and at 49 minutes, 38 seconds, Jordan says this thing and then we put it there in the email. You can’t bend the structure of reality, everything you distort snaps back. And then we can then pull the thread on the implications of what that means, which is a perfect lead in to Sphere of Influence. The whole premise of Sphere of Influence from the beginning is this idea of world building and done in a very specific way. And the way that we feel we do that as expressed inside of Sphere of Influence matches or far more closely matches reality, as opposed to this idea of these linear funnels that we feel don’t match reality.

His second thing he said was everything you distort snaps back. Well, I think every person who’s “bought” very linear, very specific funnels that eventually stop working or stop working altogether and there’s nothing you can do to get them working again, that’s part of the implications of what he said. I mean, we could unpack just that one sentence over an entire story-powered promotion, using that as the theme to attach this idea of Sphere of Influence to that. I think that’s something. That’s an example that I’ve just made up right now of something that happened to me on Saturday.

Shawn (00:56:56):

Yeah. To me, that’s the perfect example, because it happens all the time. I think what you just spoke here is the message I hope everybody heard, which is it’s two sentences. You can unpack the implications, and that’s really the word I’m glad that you drew attention to is often what we’re doing is we’re starting with something and then we’re unraveling all the implications of that something, and that’s a story.

So much of the Art of Email is based on a single observation, which is an observation of reality that many people have made. We refer to Dean Jackson about it a lot, but this has been made by infinite numbers of people who’ve looked at their data that most people who are going to buy from you are not going to buy in the first 90 days, they’re going to buy between day 91 and two years. That’s reality. That’s the equivalent of gravity in our world.

That in and of itself, it’s interesting. Oh, wow. But the Art of Email, really everything that follows the course is a byproduct of the implications of that reality. You can create an entire course on implications of a single observation, a single data point. There’s absolutely no reason that when you find something that’s interesting to you that piques your curiosity, it’s likely that will be interesting to your audience too.

And when you can unpack the implications of that as they relate and sort of set up to something that you have to offer, that’s a story-powered promotion. That’s all of it. We don’t have to make it more complicated than that. Yes, you can make it more complicated. If you’re an amazing writer and you’ve got great stories and you want to weave them together and have subtext and subplots. Sure, if you can pull all of that off, absolutely. We’re not going to stand in the way, but just realize you don’t have to.

All right. We’ll move on to the last thing here. This is going to be funny because we haven’t even had this conversation yet. We talked about it briefly on our Friday morning needing to kind of get directionally right, but we haven’t talked about this. So, this is going to be a really free one.

All right. We know we’re going to do a Black Friday promotion this year. It’s odd. Here’s the setup. We have been working on the Art of Email all year effectively. We put a stake in the ground to have it. We get to have it for sale on September 30th and have the last content delivered on October 25th, which is today when we’re recording this.

We now have between today and Black Friday 32 days to have set up a promotion, because that’s the day that the promotion will actually start. However, that’s not all we’re doing between now and then. We’re doing a significant update to another course. We have two more weeks of this workshop and content to create for that. There’s other stuff to going on and you have 32 days.

The other caveat is that we have said, I guess, two conditions. One, whatever we deliver, whatever we sell for Black Friday needs to be finished when it’s for sale. People can buy them. Whatever they’re buying, they get the whole thing. Last year we worked right up until the 20 something of December, and that’s the second caveat is we intend to take most of December off. We’re not going to be working on delivering something over the course of December, any part of it. So, lots of constraints.

I don’t think we want to reveal. Well, you tell me. Do we want to reveal what it is yet? Or do we just want to talk around? Just talk around. We won’t reveal it. We want to keep everybody excited, unless you choose to.

A couple of other things to think about too. We have two emergent marketing newsletters between now and Black Friday. So next Friday, and then the following Friday. The second one is the Friday before Thanksgiving week in the United States. That’s when Black Friday is at the end of Thanksgiving week. We can kind of conceptualize this as two emails, a PLS over the Thanksgiving week. Cart open Friday, cart close Monday. That’s sort of how we can think about it.

And we’ll do our emergent marketing newsletter, the 2021 year end review on December 3rd, which is the following week, which is a lot of work too. So, we have that to factor in. So, all of that. Anything you want to add to those, sort of the structural parameters before we get into … I have a feeling we’re going to have to talk about what it is. I don’t know, it would be curious to see how long you can talk around it.

André (01:02:12):

Well, one of the implications of what you just said is I don’t think we’re going to get much sleep between now and then.

Shawn (01:02:20):

Yeah. We haven’t for the last six weeks either.

André (01:02:25):

Yeah.

Shawn (01:02:26):

I just keep telling myself we’re taking December off. I’m like, “Oh, yeah, okay. That may not be enough.” December 3rd to January 3rd may not be enough to recover from January 1st to whatever, December 3rd, 2021.

André (01:02:44):

Yeah. What Shawn has just externalized, we send our newsletters every second week. It just so happens that there’s two between now and when it’s Black Friday, so this automatically fits in. I mean, this is going to have to fit around that. We’ll do a hard bridge in those emails. I don’t know if we’ll do one separately as a solo bridge. I don’t know. We’ll get a sense when we first mention this in the newsletter to see how excited people are for this idea. And yeah, we’re going to have to kind of mention what it is.

Shawn (01:03:39):

Yeah. Think one thing we can draw attention to, I’ll continue before the big reveal. Because we know what we’re selling and we know we have two opportunities to create a newsletter between now and then, it’s a natural way to set it up is to have the next … I think we’ve decided this. We’re going to do a themed series over the next two emails, and that theme series will, of course, be independently valuable. So, we can just consume the theme series and it’s valuable to them. There’s nothing missing from it. But that series is also going to set up. It’s going to, in many ways, frame the offer that comes next. So, it’s an opportunity to get people who are potentially interested thinking about this topic. The topic is creativity. I’m looking that way at least for now.

We’re going to do a two part series over the next two emergent marketing newsletters that emphasizes the creativity and how creativity fits into how we create content, and the role that creativity plays. This I think is really important to draw attention to. We don’t know what that means yet. We don’t have any ideas for what that means yet.

I mean, today is Monday. We check in regularly Monday mornings and Friday mornings. Probably the first time we’ll start to really think about what that series might look like, it’s obviously on both of our minds right now. But to really start getting into theme narrative arc over two VNLs, that probably will start happening on Friday. And my guess is we’ll throw out some ideas Friday. Maybe get to something that we both agree on or not. And then Monday, we need to have made that decision, because Friday we’ll be sending out the first email and we’ll be writing it next week.

We still have a little bit of time, but … That’s a typical process. I think that’s important to draw attention to. We don’t have a meeting where we’re like, “Okay, here’s what we’re going to do. This is the theme. Here are three choices. Let’s choose this one, off we go.” It’s much more organic than that. It’s almost like we’re going from an idea and then turning it into some kind of valuable asset that we can use.

André (01:06:12):

That’s an idea.

Shawn (01:06:15):

Any thoughts on that idea, André? Why don’t you run with that idea for a moment? Everyone is listening [crosstalk 01:06:23] drunk. We’re not drunk. At least not so drunk we couldn’t fish.

André (01:06:31):

I definitely want to take up fishing. That idea that I mentioned just now that something that somebody said over the weekend, I just scribbled down. And at some point, that could turn into an asset. And the asset in that example would be an entire story-powered promotion around, for example, Sphere of Influence that we could plug in to our Art of Email system that would generate revenue for us long term for years.

Just from that one little idea, me sitting, having lunch on this last Saturday and hearing him at 49 minutes say that thing. We have to create a process about going from that to the asset. And then the asset is caught an asset because it has this intrinsic value that can generate revenue for weeks, months and certainly years. So, we have a course that we created a while back. In fact, it was never released. The course doesn’t exist as one of our flagship products. We just released it as one of our Black Fridays. 2019, I think it was 2019. Yeah.

Shawn (01:07:51):

2019. Yep, [crosstalk 01:07:53].

André (01:07:53):

2019. And we did a Black Friday promotion around that. In fact, it’s inside of our product launch series module. It’s one of the things that we unpack one of our campaigns, which was that campaign. You can go and see what it is. We’ve decided we’re going to update that course now because it’s never been released and it’s just been sitting there, and we would like to work on it a bit more.

We are going to update it or add a whole bunch of our current ideas to it, and then see if anybody else is interested in that. I think we put about 170 or 180 people into that when we did that in 2017. So, we know that there’s a significant portion of our audience that don’t have it, that certainly have never even heard of it, and it could be valuable to them. So, that’s why we are going to be doing that for this Black Friday.

Shawn (01:08:52):

At the time, it was kind of a different value proposition because it was … We were not business partners, we were working as consulting partners with a client. And André had flown to New York city. We hold up in a two bedroom suite outside of New York City from Monday to, I think … No, I picked you up Sunday, and we worked Monday through Thursday and brought you back on Friday.

André (01:08:52):

On a Friday.

Shawn (01:09:16):

And what we did each day is we kind of wrote an email about what we had done that day and sort of got see the evolution of us working together to create a front end for this client. It has to remain unnamed for privacy reasons, but it was really looking at how we did that, and we recorded a … I think we had a Q&A and we recorded an overview of what … At the time, I don’t even know if we would have called it a PKM, a personal knowledge management solution or method, or whatever you would call it.

That’s an interesting precursor, but since then, of course, we’ve got 18 plus months working together collaboratively as business partners over the course of lots of updating a full course, creating two new courses. Lots have happened. We’ve written hundreds of thousands of words together. Our PKM methods have changed dramatically, but at the core, we’re still in the same business. The course was called Ideas to Assets, but we’re still in the Ideas to Assets business. We just probably understand it a lot better than we did then.

It’s exciting to think about it. In many ways, updating that course is a reward. We’re both so interested in it that it’s something to look forward to. We’re going to get to kind of capture another slice in time, update the course, put it together, put it out in the world for Black Friday. And we think our audience will be interested in it. That’s it.

We don’t know the frame yet. We don’t know the theme. We know the next two emails or the next two emergent marketing newsletters will be about creativity in some capacity, but we need to link it in a way so that we need to do two things simultaneously. For people who are not interested in Ideas to Assets, they need to have a positive experience of those two emails that has a beginning and an end, and it all makes sense and it’s congruent. They don’t need to do anything more.

And we also are going to have a subset of that audience who are going to have beginning and the end, and it’s going to open the door and position them to be … It’s the thing that they’re also interested in, the Ideas to Assets course makes sense as it continues. That’s a lot for one … Whatever, it will be four or five day total promotion to do. That’s a lot, and there’s a holiday in the middle of it and it start of the holiday shopping season and all kinds of excitement, and there’s a pandemic. What else to throw more in the mix? So, lots to consider.

From my perspective, the thing to really draw attention to for everybody listening to this is that there’s no template here. We’re figuring this out as we go along, just like you will. You kind of have a sense of where you want to go, and then you start to keep your eyes and ears open for something that might be that thing that you can hang the whole thing on.

You may hear somebody say something, you may have a thought yourself. Particularly if you go for a walk, taking a 45 minute walks are great for idea generation, but there are lots. We know it has to happen. Black Friday arrives and we haven’t done the work. We’ve missed an opportunity. We know it needs to happen and we trust that it will happen. Everything else, it’s just one foot in front of the other.

André (01:09:16):

Yeah.

Shawn (01:12:49):

Yeah. I’ll let you wrap it up with that, and then we’ll call it a day. This has been a long one, I think.

André (01:12:54):

This has been a long one. We’ve not mentioned this to anybody. So, you guys are the first ones to hear about the Black Friday thing. Not because you should go, but if you’re not one of those 170 that had the original one, but you know that we are doing this now. You can wait for us to send the emails. You can see how we’ve decided to do those, mention it inside of the value newsletter and how we’ve done the bridge. You can segment yourself out into this thing, and you can watch what we do.

We are queuing this up so you can learn from it as a component of the Art of Email course. And obviously, if you’re listed in the Black Friday promotion, there’s that too, but that’s not really the point of this. This is a learning experience for you guys. So, you get to see us doing this live now, knowing that it’s coming. So, yeah, it’s going to be fun. [crosstalk 01:13:59].

Shawn (01:14:00):

I’ve had this suspicion that we’re going to add something to the workshop, because we said five weeks and then we did it with a momentum builder workshop. Also, we had an extra week and bonus content. No promises yet, but maybe we’ll do a retrospective. It would be interesting for them to see the setup for a PLS that’s coming and here today. And then they can experience the whole thing.

Maybe we’ll do a retrospective that we look back on it and sort of … Then they see three perspectives. Before it happened, after it happened, while it happened. No promises. That sounds like it might be fun. We always want to over deliver. I suspect this will find its way into the workshop before too long. Who knows, maybe 2022 though. Excellent. All right. Yeah. All right. As always, thanks for listening. And like we keep saying, get back to work.
